[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")

Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.

The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")

revision.text:
[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化|12=draft}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市|12=draft}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川|12=draft}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|12=draft}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|12=draft}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|12=draft}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|12=draft}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|12=draft}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|12=draft}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代|12=draft}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|12=draft}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚|12=draft}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣|12=draft}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ|12=draft}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器|12=draft}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀|12=draft}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀|12=draft}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=draft}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化|12=draft}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館|12=draft}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚|12=draft}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく|12=draft}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川|12=draft}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|12=draft}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|12=draft}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|12=draft}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|12=draft}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|12=draft}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|12=draft}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|12=draft}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代|12=draft}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|12=draft}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚|12=draft}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣|12=draft}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ|12=draft}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器|12=draft}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器|12=draft}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀|12=draft}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀|12=draft}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=draft}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化|12=draft}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館|12=draft}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.

Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=draft}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化|12=draft}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館|12=draft}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚|12=draft}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|12=draft}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく|12=draft}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

revision.text:
[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|モヨロ貝塚（2010年4月）]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = モヨロ貝塚| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''モヨロ貝塚'''（モヨロかいづか）は、[[北海道]][[網走市]]北1条から北3条東2丁目にかけてある[[オホーツク文化]]の代表的[[遺跡]]である。[[網走川]]河口左岸、[[オホーツク海]]のそばに位置する。国の[[史跡]]に指定されている（指定名称は'''最寄貝塚'''）<ref group="注">北海道では、他に以下の貝塚が国の史跡に指定されている。 *[[東釧路貝塚]] 釧路市 1970年（昭和45年）指定 *[[北黄金貝塚]] 伊達市 1987年（昭和62年）指定 *[[入江貝塚]] 虻田郡洞爺湖町 1988年（昭和63年）指定 *[[大谷地貝塚]] 余市郡余市町 2000年（平成12年）指定</ref>。本貝塚名は、当時の[[アイヌ]]人がモヨロ・コタンと呼んでいたことから、1918年（大正7年）に[[米村喜男衛]]によって付けられた。モヨロは[[アイヌ語]]で「入江の内、あるいは所」という意味<ref name="takeda">竹田輝雄「最寄貝塚」/文化庁文化財保護部史跡研究会監修『図説 日本の史跡 第1巻 原始1』同朋舎出版 1991年 30ページ</ref>。
== 概要 == 本貝塚は、[[網走川]]河口の左岸（北岸）にあり、標高5メートルの砂丘台地に立地する。[[編年]]的に日本の[[縄文時代]]晩期に並行する時期から建物が作られ、[[続縄文時代]]が続いた。さらにオホーツク文化に変わっても集落が営まれた。[[住居]]は[[竪穴建物]]で、死者は[[貝塚]]に埋葬された。
オホーツク文化の大型建物には、[[海獣]]、[[ヒグマ]]などの骨が丁寧に並べられていた。貝塚からは[[屈葬]]された人骨が多数見つかった。多数出土した物には[[骨角器]]、[[土器]]、[[石器]]があり、また本州で制作されたとみられる[[鉄]]の[[刀]]（[[直刀]]・[[蕨手刀]]・毛抜形太刀など）や[[鉾]]、大陸から持ち込まれたとみられる[[青銅]]の[[鈴]]などがあった。土器や骨角器には[[クジラ]]・[[イルカ]]、クマの彫刻が見られる。牙で熊など動物をかたどった像があり、中には優れた造形の牙製女性像もある<ref group="注">沿海州から伝播したとみられる[[セイウチ]]の牙を彫った婦人像</ref><ref name="takeda"/>。道具類の比重から海獣の狩猟に重点があったと推測されている。
== 発掘史 == [[1913年]]（[[大正]]2年）に網走を訪れた[[青森県]]のアマチュア考古学研究者[[米村喜男衛]]が発見し、学界に報告した。発見した土器<ref group="注">[[河野広道]]が[[樺太]]、北海道オホーツク沿岸や[[千島]]出土の土器群とモヨロ貝塚出土の土器を'''オホーツク式土器群'''として四型式に分類した。</ref><ref name="dokigun">和田英昭「モヨロ貝塚」 小林達雄編『考古学ハンドブック』新書館 2007年1月 247ページ</ref>から[[縄文文化]]とも[[アイヌ文化]]とも異なる文化の存在を知った米村は、網走に住むことを決めて米村理髪店を開業し、傍らで遺跡の調査と研究に携わった。
大正時代には、この遺跡の文化が北方的な独特のものであるということ以上はわからなかった。[[1933年]]（[[昭和]]8年）に、オホーツク海の南沿岸に広がるオホーツク沿岸文化が、同時代の北海道の文化と別個のものとみなされるようになった。今日いうオホーツク文化である。
遺物を保存・展示するために、[[1936年]]（[[昭和]]11年）に網走に北見郷土館（現 [[網走市立郷土博物館]]）が建てられた。モヨロ貝塚は同年[[12月16日]]に国の史跡に指定された<ref>{{国指定文化財等データベース|401|22|最寄貝塚}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 昭和11年文部省告示第367号『官報』第2988号、p.518]</ref>。[[1941年]]（昭和16年）・[[1942年]]（昭和17年）、海軍施設建設のため遺跡の一部が破壊され、緊急発掘を受けた。100体を超える人骨と多量の土器、石器、骨角器、金属器などが出土した。北海道大学医学部・大場利夫によって資料報告（『北方文化研究報告』北海道大学・[[1956年]]（昭和31年）および以降）されている<ref name="takeda"/>。
史跡指定時28棟確認されていた建物跡は、このため現在には約20棟に減った。戦後、[[1947年]]（昭和22年）から[[1951年]]（昭和26年）にかけて大規模な調査が実施された。平成期はじめの発掘調査で約80基ほどの墓が密集して発見され、また[[大麦]]はじめ多くの栽培植物の種子が見つかっている<ref name="dokigun"/>。
== 現況 == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|モヨロ貝塚館]] [[File:2013年4月モヨロ貝塚館.jpg|thumb|240px|2013年5月改築後]] 遺跡はモヨロ貝塚館を中心にした公園として整備されている。北見郷土館の後進である[[網走市立郷土博物館]]にも展示がある。[[2003年]]（[[平成]]15年）と[[2004年]]（平成16年）に発掘が行われ、土製のクマ像など多数の遺物が見つかった。
「モヨロ貝塚館」は建て替えられ、[[2013年]]（平成25年）[[5月1日]]に新館が開館した<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">山田泰雄（2014年5月2日）. “モヨロ貝塚館:新装オープン 展示場7倍に−−網走”. [[毎日新聞]]（毎日新聞社）</ref>。
== 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} === 注釈 === {{Reflist|group="注"}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == *米村喜男衛『モヨロ貝塚資料集』、網走市立郷土博物館・野村書店、1950年 *[[駒井和愛]]編『オホーツク海沿岸・知床半島の遺跡 下巻』、[[東京大学文学部]]、1964年 *米村衛『北辺の海の民 モヨロ貝塚』、新泉社、2004年、ISBN 4787704311
== 関連項目 == *[[貝塚]] *[[オロチョンの火祭り]] *[[日本の貝塚一覧]] *[[街道をゆく]] - 「オホーツク街道」米村の事績について詳しい。
== 外部リンク == * {{Wayback|url=http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ |title=網走市立郷土博物館友の会 |date=20100101000000}} * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm モヨロ貝塚]
{{考古学}} {{日本の文化財}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もよろかいつか}} [[Category:日本の貝塚]] [[Category:オホーツク文化]] [[Category:北海道の考古遺跡]] [[Category:北海道にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:網走市の歴史]] [[Category:北見国]]

{{考古学}} {{日本の文化財}} {{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:もよろかいつか}} [[Category:日本の貝塚]] [[Category:オホーツク文化]] [[Category:北海道の考古遺跡]] [[Category:北海道にある国指定の史跡]] [[Category:網走市の歴史]] [[Category:北見国]]

[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化|qq=draft}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市|qq=draft}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川|qq=draft}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|qq=draft}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|qq=draft}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|qq=draft}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|qq=draft}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|qq=draft}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|qq=draft}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代|qq=draft}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|qq=draft}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚|qq=draft}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣|qq=draft}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ|qq=draft}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器|qq=draft}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀|qq=draft}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀|qq=draft}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|qq=draft}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化|qq=draft}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館|qq=draft}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|qq=draft}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく|qq=draft}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

<!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound-->

revision.comment: Bot: remove sole member from [[Category:Moyoro Shell Mound]]
revision.text:
[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|ja|オホーツク文化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okhotsk culture|qq=}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri, Hokkaido|ja|網走市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abashiri, Hokkaido|qq=}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|ja|網走川|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abashiri River|qq=}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|qq=}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita Kogan Shell Midden|qq=}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irie Shell Midden|qq=}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otayachi Shell Midden|qq=}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonezawa Kioe|qq=}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|ja|アイヌ語|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu language|qq=}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jomon period|ja|縄文時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon period|qq=}}, continuing through the {{ill|Epi-Jomon culture|ja|続縄文時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Epi-Jomon culture|qq=}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit dwellings|qq=}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|shell midden|ja|貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/shell midden|qq=}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|marine mammals|ja|海獣|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/marine mammals|qq=}} and {{ill|brown bears|ja|ヒグマ|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/brown bears|qq=}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|bone tools|ja|骨角器|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/bone tools|qq=}}, {{ill|pottery|ja|土器|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/pottery|qq=}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|ja|直刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chokutō|qq=}}, {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warabite-to|qq=}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mounuki-type swords|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon culture|qq=}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|ja|アイヌ文化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ainu culture|qq=}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri Municipal Museum|ja|網走市立郷土博物館|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Abashiri Municipal Museum|qq=}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Shell midden|ja|貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shell midden|qq=}} * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orochon Fire Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shell middens in Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaido o Yuku|qq=}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]

[[sn:Moyoro Shell Mound]] [[ja:モヨロ貝塚]] [[en:Moyoro Shell Mound]]
<!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--><!--Moyoro Shell Mound--> [[Category:オホーツク文化]]

revision.comment: Bot: add category Category:北海道にある国指定の史跡
revision.text:
[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative [[:en:Okhotsk culture|Okhotsk culture]] archaeological site located in [[:en:Abashiri|Abashiri, Hokkaido]], extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the [[:en:Abashiri River|Abashiri River]] near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|qq=}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita Kogan Shell Midden|qq=}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irie Shell Midden|qq=}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otayachi Shell Midden|qq=}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonezawa Kioe|qq=}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in [[:en:Ainu language|Ainu language]].<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late [[:en:Jōmon period|Jomon period]], continuing through the [[:en:Zoku-Jōmon period|Epi-Jomon culture]] and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit dwellings|qq=}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the [[:en:Midden|shell midden]].
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of [[:en:Marine mammal|marine mammals]] and [[:en:Brown bear|brown bears]], suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including [[:en:Bone tool|bone tools]], [[:en:Earthenware|pottery]], and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., [[:en:Chokutō|Chokutō]], {{ill|warabite-to|ja|蕨手刀|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warabite-to|qq=}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mounuki-type swords|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon culture|qq=}} and [[:en:Ainu culture|Ainu culture]] based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now [[:en:Abashiri City Folk Museum|Abashiri Municipal Museum]]), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>
== References == {{Reflist}} === Notes === {{Reflist|group="note"}}
== Further Reading == * Yonezawa Kioe, ''Moyoro Kaizuka Materials Collection,'' Abashiri Municipal Museum and Nomura Shoten, 1950. * Komai Kazue, ed., ''Okhotsk Coastal Sites and Shiretoko Peninsula, Vol. 2,'' Tokyo University Faculty of Letters, 1964. * Yonezawa Ei, ''People of the Northern Seas: Moyoro Shell Midden,'' Shinzen Publishing, 2004. ISBN 4787704311.
== Related Articles == * [[:en:Midden|Shell midden]] * {{ill|Orochon Fire Festival|ja|オロチョンの火祭り|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Orochon Fire Festival|qq=}} * {{ill|List of shell middens in Japan|ja|日本の貝塚一覧|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of shell middens in Japan|qq=}} * {{ill|Kaido o Yuku|ja|街道をゆく|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaido o Yuku|qq=}} (Detailed discussion on Yonezawa's work in "Okhotsk Kaido.")
== External Links == * [http://sapporo.cool.ne.jp/hakubutukanntomono/ Abashiri Municipal Museum Friends Association (Archived)] * [http://inoues.net/ruins/moyoro_kaiduka.htm Moyoro Shell Midden]
{{Cultural Properties of Japan}} {{Normdaten}}
{{translated page|ja|モヨロ貝塚|version=95997137|comment=Imported full ja history}}
