== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|WD=Q52824|lt=Jomon period|1=Jōmon period}}, continuing through the {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|WD=Q11607084|lt=Epi-Jomon culture|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit dwellings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q940435}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|Midden|en|Midden|WD=Q1152199|lt=shell midden|1=Midden}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|Marine mammal|en|Marine mammal|WD=Q382441|lt=marine mammals|1=Marine mammal}} and {{ill|Brown bear|en|Brown bear|WD=Q36341|lt=brown bears|1=Brown bear}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|Bone tool|en|Bone tool|WD=Q814769|lt=bone tools|1=Bone tool}}, {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|WD=Q1377111|lt=pottery|1=Earthenware}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|WD=Q63821|lt=Chokutō|1=Chokutō}}, {{ill|warabite Katana|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warabite Katana|qq=}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mounuki-type swords|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon culture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 縄文時代|1=Jōmon period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q52824}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|en|Ainu culture|WD=Q11282348|lt=Ainu culture|1=Ainu culture}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri City Folk Museum|en|Abashiri City Folk Museum|WD=Q11607220|lt=Abashiri Municipal Museum|1=Abashiri City Folk Museum}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

ISBN 4787704311.

[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|en|Okhotsk culture|qid=Q1093651|lt=Okhotsk culture|1=Okhotsk culture}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri|en|Abashiri|qid=Q305640|lt=Abashiri, Hokkaido|1=Abashiri}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|en|Abashiri River|qid=Q3650456|lt=Abashiri River|1=Abashiri River}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita Kogan Shell Midden|1=Kitakogane Shell Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q103810663}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irie Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 入江・高砂貝塚|1=Irie-Takasago Shell Mounds|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q103813700}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otayachi Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonezawa Kioe|1=Yonemura Kioe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15077151}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|qid=Q52824|lt=Jomon period|1=Jōmon period}}, continuing through the {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|qid=Q11607084|lt=Epi-Jomon culture|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit dwellings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|Midden|en|Midden|qid=Q1152199|lt=shell midden|1=Midden}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|Marine mammal|en|Marine mammal|qid=Q382441|lt=marine mammals|1=Marine mammal}} and {{ill|Brown bear|en|Brown bear|qid=Q36341|lt=brown bears|1=Brown bear}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|Bone tool|en|Bone tool|qid=Q814769|lt=bone tools|1=Bone tool}}, {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|qid=Q1377111|lt=pottery|1=Earthenware}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|qid=Q63821|lt=Chokutō|1=Chokutō}}, {{ill|warabite Katana|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warabite Katana|qq=}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mounuki-type swords|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon culture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 縄文時代|1=Jōmon period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52824}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|en|Ainu culture|qid=Q11282348|lt=Ainu culture|1=Ainu culture}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri City Folk Museum|en|Abashiri City Folk Museum|qid=Q11607220|lt=Abashiri Municipal Museum|1=Abashiri City Folk Museum}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

revision.text:
[[File:Moyoro Shell Midden.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden (April 2010)]] {{Coord|44|1|30.0|N|144|16|3.7|E|region:JP-01|display=title}} {{Location map|Japan Hokkaido|relief=1| lat_deg = 44| lat_min = 1| lat_sec = 30.0| lon_deg = 144| lon_min = 16| lon_sec = 3.7| label = Moyoro Shell Midden| position = top| mark = Red pog.svg| marksize = 10| width = 240| caption={{Center|}}}} '''Moyoro Shell Midden''' (モヨロ貝塚, Moyoro Kaizuka) is a representative {{ill|Okhotsk culture|en|Okhotsk culture|qid=Q1093651|lt=Okhotsk culture|1=Okhotsk culture}} archaeological site located in {{ill|Abashiri|en|Abashiri|qid=Q305640|lt=Abashiri, Hokkaido|1=Abashiri}}, extending from Kita 1-chome to Kita 3-chome Higashi 2-chome. Situated on the left bank of the {{ill|Abashiri River|en|Abashiri River|qid=Q3650456|lt=Abashiri River|1=Abashiri River}} near the Sea of Okhotsk, it is designated as a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|national historic site]] (registered as '''Saiyo Shell Midden''').<ref group="note">In Hokkaido, other shell middens designated as national historic sites include: * {{ill|Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja|東釧路貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Higashi Kushiro Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Kushiro City, designated in 1970) * {{ill|Kita Kogan Shell Midden|ja|北黄金貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kita Kogan Shell Midden|1=Kitakogane Shell Mound|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q103810663}} (Date City, designated in 1987) * {{ill|Irie Shell Midden|ja|入江貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Irie Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 入江・高砂貝塚|1=Irie-Takasago Shell Mounds|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q103813700}} (Toyako Town, designated in 1988) * {{ill|Otayachi Shell Midden|ja|大谷地貝塚|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Otayachi Shell Midden|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (Yoichi Town, designated in 2000)</ref> The name "Moyoro" was given by amateur archaeologist {{ill|Yonezawa Kioe|ja|米村喜男衛|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yonezawa Kioe|1=Yonemura Kioe|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15077151}} in 1918, inspired by the term "Moyoro Kotan," meaning "inlet place" in {{ill|Ainu language|en|Ainu language|qid=Q27969|lt=Ainu language|1=Ainu language}}.<ref name="takeda">Takeda Teruo, "Saiyo Shell Midden," in Bunkachō Bunka Zaihō-bu Shiseki Kenkyū-kai, ed., ''Illustrated History of Japan, Volume 1: Prehistory 1,'' Dōbōsha Publishing, 1991, p. 30.</ref>
== Overview == The site is located on a sandy plateau about five meters above sea level on the northern bank of the Abashiri River's mouth. Buildings were constructed during a period corresponding to the late {{ill|Jōmon period|en|Jōmon period|qid=Q52824|lt=Jomon period|1=Jōmon period}}, continuing through the {{ill|Zoku-Jōmon period|en|Zoku-Jōmon period|qid=Q11607084|lt=Epi-Jomon culture|1=Zoku-Jōmon period}} and into the Okhotsk culture. {{ill|Pit dwellings|ja|竪穴建物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Pit dwellings|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 竪穴式住居|1=Pit-house|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q940435}} were used as residences, while the deceased were buried in the {{ill|Midden|en|Midden|qid=Q1152199|lt=shell midden|1=Midden}}.
Large buildings associated with the Okhotsk culture contained arranged remains of {{ill|Marine mammal|en|Marine mammal|qid=Q382441|lt=marine mammals|1=Marine mammal}} and {{ill|Brown bear|en|Brown bear|qid=Q36341|lt=brown bears|1=Brown bear}}, suggesting ceremonial or ritualistic practices. The shell midden has yielded numerous artifacts, including {{ill|Bone tool|en|Bone tool|qid=Q814769|lt=bone tools|1=Bone tool}}, {{ill|Earthenware|en|Earthenware|qid=Q1377111|lt=pottery|1=Earthenware}}, and stone implements. Artifacts of non-local origin include iron swords (e.g., {{ill|Chokutō|en|Chokutō|qid=Q63821|lt=Chokutō|1=Chokutō}}, {{ill|warabite Katana|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/warabite Katana|qq=}}, and {{ill|mounuki-type swords|ja|毛抜形太刀|1=Kenukigata-tachi|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/mounuki-type swords|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata-tachi|qq=}}) and bronze bells believed to have been imported from the mainland. Artworks such as whale and bear carvings on tools and finely crafted ivory figurines, including a detailed walrus ivory female figure, have also been discovered.<ref group="note">The walrus ivory figurine is thought to have been introduced from the Primorsky Krai region.</ref><ref name="takeda"/> The predominance of tools suggests that marine mammal hunting was a central activity.
== Excavation History == The site was discovered in 1913 by amateur archaeologist Yonezawa Kioe, who reported its significance to academia. Yonezawa identified a cultural context distinct from {{ill|Jomon culture|ja|縄文文化|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jomon culture|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 縄文時代|1=Jōmon period|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52824}} and {{ill|Ainu culture|en|Ainu culture|qid=Q11282348|lt=Ainu culture|1=Ainu culture}} based on the discovered pottery.<ref group="note">Hiromichi Kawano classified pottery from Sakhalin, the Okhotsk coast, and the Kuril Islands, along with those from the Moyoro Shell Midden, into the ''Okhotsk-style pottery group'' with four types.</ref><ref name="dokigun">Wada Hideaki, "Moyoro Shell Midden," in Kobayashi Tatsuo, ed., ''Archaeological Handbook,'' Shinshokan, January 2007, p. 247.</ref>
In 1933, the unique "Okhotsk coastal culture" was recognized as distinct from contemporary Hokkaido cultures, later identified as the Okhotsk culture. A museum, the North Kitami Historical Museum (now {{ill|Abashiri City Folk Museum|en|Abashiri City Folk Museum|qid=Q11607220|lt=Abashiri Municipal Museum|1=Abashiri City Folk Museum}}), was established in 1936 to preserve artifacts. The site was designated a national historic site on December 16, 1936.<ref>{{National Cultural Properties Database|401|22|Saiyo Shell Midden}}</ref><ref>[https://dl.ndl.go.jp/info:ndljp/pid/2959470/2 Ministry of Education Announcement No. 367, Showa Year 11, in ''Kanpō'' Issue 2988, p. 518]</ref>
During WWII, parts of the site were destroyed for naval construction, prompting emergency excavations. Over 100 human remains and numerous artifacts were recovered. Further excavations in the late 20th and early 21st centuries uncovered additional graves and cultivated plant seeds, including barley.<ref name="dokigun"/>
== Current Status == [[File:Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|right|Moyoro Shell Midden Museum]] [[File:2013 Moyoro Shell Midden Museum.jpg|thumb|240px|Museum after its 2013 renovation]] The site has been developed into a park centered around the Moyoro Shell Midden Museum. Artifacts are also displayed at the Abashiri Municipal Museum. Recent excavations in 2003–2004 uncovered items such as earthen bear figurines. A new Moyoro Shell Midden Museum opened on May 1, 2013.<ref name="mainichi-np-2014-5-2">Yamada Yasuo (May 2, 2014). "New Moyoro Shell Midden Museum Opens: Exhibition Space Expands Sevenfold in Abashiri." ''Mainichi Shimbun'' (The Mainichi Newspapers).</ref>

ISBN 4787704311.
